All Questions
Were You Asked This?
Design a system to rollout new versions of a mobile OS to devices worldwide

Design a system that can securely distribute mobile OS updates to millions of devices worldwide, handling different device types, network conditions, and rollout strategies like staged deployments.

Asked at:

Amazon

Google

Microsoft

Over‑the‑air (OTA) Update Service is a secure, planet‑scale distribution and orchestration system that delivers new versions of a mobile OS to millions of devices. Users see a notification, download the update under diverse network conditions, and install it safely with the ability to pause, resume, or defer. Release managers configure staged rollouts, target cohorts (model, region, carrier), and monitor health to pause or roll back.

Interviewers ask this because it blends control-plane orchestration with data-plane content delivery, touches on security and trust, and stresses capacity planning and safety. Strong answers show you can separate concerns, design for unreliable networks, prevent thundering herds, build feedback loops for staged deployments, and protect the supply chain with signatures and attestation.

Community Solutions
Design a system to rollout new versions of a mobile OS to devices worldwide
by WillowyAmaranthShrimp324
6
5
Design a system to rollout new versions of a mobile OS to devices worldwide
by Abhishek Athreya
1
7
Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to receive update availability based on eligibility (device model, region, carrier) and rollout wave.

Users should be able to securely download and install updates with integrity verification, pause/resume, and automatic retry on flaky networks.

Release managers should be able to configure staged rollouts, targeting rules, and have a global pause/rollback switch.

Users should be able to see reliable progress and recover safely from failures without bricking the device.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How would you implement staged rollouts with a global kill switch while avoiding a thundering herd on release day?

How do you guarantee end‑to‑end security and integrity of the update so a compromised server or network can’t deliver a malicious OS?

How would you deliver multi‑gigabyte images efficiently over unreliable mobile networks (roaming, metered, intermittent Wi‑Fi)?

What telemetry and health signals would you collect to auto‑pause a bad rollout and how do you close the loop quickly?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Handling Large Blobs

OS images are multi‑GB artifacts. Efficient, resumable, chunked delivery with CDN caching and delta updates is central to meeting cost, reliability, and user‑experience goals.

Read More
Multi-step Processes

An update spans eligibility, download, verify, pre‑install checks, reboot, and post‑install health. Modeling this as a workflow with explicit states and compensation (rollback) avoids bricking devices.

Read More
Dealing with Contention

Release events can trigger millions of simultaneous requests. Cohorting, rate limiting, and backoff are required to prevent overload of control endpoints and CDNs.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Kafka

Kafka provides durable, scalable ingestion of device telemetry and rollout events. It enables real‑time aggregation (failure rates, anomalies) to drive automated pause/rollback decisions.

Read More
Cassandra

A globally distributed, write‑heavy device registry needs low‑latency reads/writes at scale. Cassandra suits storing per‑device state (eligibility, rollout cohort, last seen build) with high availability.

Read More
API Gateway

An API Gateway cleanly fronts device check‑in and eligibility APIs with authentication, rate limits, and canary routing. It simplifies gradual rollout of control‑plane changes across regions.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

Dropbox

Both deliver large files reliably over the public internet with resume, deduplication, and CDN caching; OTA adds stronger integrity guarantees and orchestrated install workflows.

Read
Practice
Job Scheduler

Modeling an update as a long, multi‑step job with retries, timeouts, and compensation mirrors a distributed scheduler’s workflow orchestration and state management.

Read
Practice
Rate Limiter

Staged rollouts rely on global and per‑region rate limits to prevent overload and manage safe ramp‑ups, similar to enforcing distributed quotas under bursty demand.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Treating the problem as simple file hosting without staged rollout and rate control

This leads to thundering herds, CDN/egress blowups, and uneven regional impact on carriers/ISPs. It also removes the safety needed to pause when issues surface.

Weak supply‑chain security (no signed manifests, no rollback protection, no pinning)

A compromised network or server could push malicious images or downgrade devices. Without a robust chain of trust, you risk mass compromise or permanent device damage.

No real‑time telemetry and auto‑pause feedback loop

If you can’t see install failures and health regressions quickly, you’ll continue to expand a bad release. This turns isolated issues into global outages and support crises.
